lessons.lessonName: Switching to Insert Mode: Insert Missing Comma
lessons.techniqueExplanation: Vim operates using distinct modes. In this lesson, you learn to switch to Insert mode by pressing 'i' and insert a missing comma within a line of code. This technique is ideal for making precise inline corrections without disrupting your workflow.
lessons.codeInstructions: Place the cursor between the two function parameters. Press 'i' to enter Insert mode, insert a comma between the parameters, and then press Esc to return to Normal mode. Ensure that the updated comma is directly after the a.
lessons.initialCode: function multiply(a b) { return a * b; }
lessons.finalCode: function multiply(a, b) { return a * b; }

lessons.lessonName: Appending Text: Insert semi colon after
lessons.techniqueExplanation: This lesson explains how Vim's append command ('a') works. When you press 'a', Vim enters insert mode, allowing you to add text immediately after the cursor's current position. This technique is ideal for inserting text after words or lines.
lessons.codeInstructions: Edit the code by inserting a semi colon at the end of the line

lessons.lessonName: Opening a New Line: Add Statement Below
lessons.techniqueExplanation: In this lesson, you'll learn how to efficiently insert a new line in Vim using the 'o' command. When you press 'o', Vim opens a new line directly below the current line and automatically enters insert mode, allowing you to start typing immediately. This technique is ideal for quickly adding code without manually repositioning the cursor.
lessons.codeInstructions:
Edit the initial code by adding a new paragraph element (

New statement

) directly below the existing

Welcome

element within the
block.

lessons.techniqueExplanation: This lesson demonstrates how to use vim’s navigation commands to quickly edit a line. The I command moves the cursor to the beginning of the line in insert mode, and the A command jumps to the end of the line in insert mode. Make sure you are in normal mode and pressing shift!
lessons.codeInstructions:
Add a

tag for the title and a

tag for the paragraph. Then new lines should look like

Title!

and

Paragraph!

lessons.lessonName: Deleting a Character: Remove Unwanted Symbol
lessons.techniqueExplanation: This lesson demonstrates how Vim's 'x' command works to delete the character under the cursor. When you're in normal mode, simply position the cursor on an unwanted character and use the command to remove it. This method is especially useful for fixing minor errors like extra quotes or typos in your code.
lessons.codeInstructions: Edit the code by removing the extra double quote between 'Sample' and 'image' in the alt attribute.

lessons.lessonName: Deleting a Whole Line: Remove Debug Statement
lessons.techniqueExplanation: In Vim's Normal mode, you can delete an entire line by positioning your cursor on that line and pressing 'dd'. This command is ideal for quickly removing unwanted code, such as debug statements, making your code cleaner and more efficient. Use this technique when you need to eliminate a line without affecting the rest of your file.
lessons.codeInstructions: Edit the code by removing the line that contains the debug print statement so that only the production code remains.
lessons.initialCode:
console.log("Debug: Value is", value);
console.log("Production code starts here");

lessons.lessonName: Cut and Paste: Reposition a Function Call
lessons.techniqueExplanation: This lesson explains how to use vim commands to reposition lines of code efficiently. In vim, you use 'dd' to cut (delete) an entire line, which removes it from its current location, and 'p' to paste it after the cursor. This technique is ideal for reorganizing your code by moving function calls or statements to better suit your program's flow, enhancing readability and structure.
lessons.codeInstructions: Move the debug statement below the risky_operation

lessons.lessonName: Yank and Paste: Duplicate a Configuration Line
lessons.techniqueExplanation: Vim lets you quickly copy an entire line using 'yy' (yank) and then paste it with 'p'. This is especially useful for duplicating configuration entries—such as server settings—so you don't have to retype similar code. Use this technique when you need to replicate a configuration line to create another similar entry in your code.
lessons.codeInstructions: Update the code by duplicating the existing server configuration object so that the array contains two identical server entries.

lessons.lessonName: Replace Character: Fix a Typo in Keyword
lessons.techniqueExplanation: The 'r' command in Normal mode replaces exactly one character under the cursor without entering Insert mode. It is ideal for fixing tiny typos quickly while keeping your editing flow fast.
lessons.codeInstructions: Fix the typo in the keyword by changing 'returb' to 'return' using a single-character replacement.
lessons.initialCode: function add(x, y) { returb x + y; }
lessons.finalCode: function add(x, y) { return x + y; }

lessons.lessonName: Change Word: Rename a Misspelled Variable
lessons.techniqueExplanation: Use 'cw' in Normal mode to change from the cursor to the end of the current word, then type the replacement. This is a fast way to correct misspelled variable names.
lessons.codeInstructions: Rename the misspelled variable from 'userNmae' to 'userName'.

lessons.lessonName: Delete Word: Remove a Duplicate Token
lessons.techniqueExplanation: The 'dw' command deletes from the cursor to the start of the next word. It is perfect for removing extra words that accidentally appear in conditions or expressions.
lessons.codeInstructions: Remove the extra word so the condition is valid.

lessons.lessonName: Change Inside Quotes: Rewrite a String
lessons.techniqueExplanation: The text object command 'ci"' changes everything inside double quotes and enters Insert mode. This lets you replace string contents without touching the surrounding quotes.
lessons.codeInstructions: Replace the text inside the quotes so the message becomes "Hello world".

lessons.techniqueExplanation: Use 'ci(' to replace everything inside parentheses while preserving the parentheses themselves. This is useful when rewriting function arguments.
lessons.codeInstructions: Replace the function arguments so the call becomes sum(total, tax).

lessons.lessonName: Find Character and Append: Fix URL Scheme
lessons.techniqueExplanation: Character find motions like 'f' and 't' let you jump directly to punctuation on a line. Combined with append ('a'), they are great for precise inline fixes.
lessons.codeInstructions: Insert the missing colon so the URL uses "https://".

lessons.lessonName: Repeat Last Change: Camel Case Two Names
lessons.techniqueExplanation: After making one edit, pressing '.' repeats the last change. This is powerful for applying the same transformation to similar lines without retyping the full command.
lessons.codeInstructions: Convert both variable names to camelCase.

lessons.techniqueExplanation: Pressing 'J' in Normal mode joins the current line with the next one, inserting clean spacing. It is useful when code is wrapped across lines and should be compacted.
lessons.codeInstructions: Join the wrapped expression into one line with proper spacing.

lessons.lessonName: Search Next Match: Edit the Second Occurrence
lessons.techniqueExplanation: Search with '/' to jump to a match, then use 'n' to move to the next occurrence. This is ideal when you need to edit only a specific repeated instance.
lessons.codeInstructions: Change only the second color value from 'red' to 'green'.

lessons.lessonName: Substitute Across File: Bump API Version
lessons.techniqueExplanation: The substitute command can replace text efficiently. Using '%s/old/new/g' applies the change globally across the whole file, which is great for version updates.
